nativeRange.habitat: Reef slopes, especially in areas where Fungiidae corals are common; free-living colonies rest on sandy or rubble substrate

decorPreferences: clear surrounding space for tentacle extension

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Target feeding of small meaty foods — micro-plankton, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or finely chopped marine foods — roughly once weekly has been reported to improve growth and coloration. Tentacles tend to be extended during daylight and can actively capture zooplankton.

avoidWith: other corals placed within tentacle reach
Fungia sp. and Heliofungia sp. without adequate spacing

summary: Polyphyllia talpina is a free-living Fungiidae LPS coral with an elongated, multi-mouthed disc and numerous white-tipped tentacles. It rests on the sand bed and is generally considered intermediate to keep; requires moderate light, low flow, and weekly target feeding.
careNotes:
Polyphyllia talpina (Lamarck, 1801) is the sole species in genus Polyphyllia and a distinctive member of family Fungiidae. Unlike the single-mouthed disc shape of Fungia and Heliofungia, Polyphyllia forms an elongated (oval, Y-, T-, or X-shaped) free-living colony with many evenly distributed mouths scattered across its upper surface — a trait reflected in the genus name ('many-leafed'). In the wild, colonies have been recorded reaching up to 75 cm (WoRMS/FAO), though aquarium specimens are typically 10–40 cm.

Key care points:
• Placement: Position directly on open sandy substrate in the lower third of the aquarium. The colony is free-living and does not attach to hard substrate; placing it on rock will stress or damage it. Leave at least 15–20 cm clear space around the specimen, as short white-tipped tentacles can sting neighboring corals.
• Lighting: Moderate intensity is generally recommended, in the range of 75–150 PAR. Note: LiveAquaria lists lighting as 'High' for this species, while the coral's natural placement on sandy reef slopes (which receives less direct illumination than upper rockwork) and its close relationship to Fungia suggest moderate PAR is more appropriate. Acclimate gradually to the target light level.
• Flow: Low to gentle indirect flow is preferred. The colony's broad fleshy surface can be damaged by direct high-velocity water movement. Gentle randomised tumbling flow is suitable; avoid direct laminar streams.
• Feeding: Has been observed responding well to weekly target-feeding with small meaty foods (brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, micro-plankton) delivered when tentacles are extended — typically during daylight hours. Photosynthesis via zooxanthellae provides a substantial share of metabolic energy under adequate lighting.
• Aggression: Despite the relatively short tentacles, this coral has been reported to damage neighboring corals on contact and is generally considered aggressive due to its sessile feeding posture with frequently extended tentacles. It cannot move away from conflicts as readily as the more mobile Fungia species.
• Distinct from siblings: Polyphyllia talpina is distinguished from the Short Tentacle Plate Coral (Fungia sp.) by its elongated multi-polyp colony form and from the Long Tentacle Plate Coral (Heliofungia actiniformis) by its multiple mouths and shorter, more numerous tentacles. All three belong to Fungiidae and share similar water parameter needs.
• Water chemistry: Standard reef parameters generally support healthy calcification. Calcium 400–450 ppm, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, and magnesium 1250–1350 ppm are broadly appropriate targets extrapolated from published Fungiidae care data; exact PPM requirements have not been independently documented for this species in multiple hobby sources.
• Cross-source disagreement: LiveAquaria describes care level as 'Easy'; ReefChasers rates comparable Fungiidae corals as 'Advanced'. Controller specification and broad consensus suggest 'Intermediate' reflects the realistic level of attention required for stable long-term maintenance. Temperature range averaged between sources (LiveAquaria 22–26°C, ReefChasers 25–29°C); final range 23–27°C.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. Captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the home aquarium hobby. Unlike branching corals, fragging is not a practical propagation method for free-living Fungiidae colonies; propagation typically occurs through natural fission in wild populations or controlled aquaculture settings.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Slipper / Tongue Coral
primary.notes: Primary care source. Provided: scientific name (Polyphyllia sp.), care level (Easy), temperament (Aggressive), temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, lighting (High), flow (Medium), placement (Bottom/sand), feeding weekly with micro-plankton or brine shrimp. Note: World Wide Corals (slice primary) did not have a Polyphyllia talpina page; LiveAquaria used as primary per fallback rules.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Fungia Plate Corals Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-check for Fungiidae family parameters. Temperature 77–84°F (25–28.9°C), pH 8.0–8.4, alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, lighting 150–200 PAR, flow low-moderate, placement sand bed. Care level rated Advanced for Fungiidae (outlier vs LA Easy); final set to Intermediate. Temperature averaged with LiveAquaria values. Guide covers Fungia specifically; applied with note that Polyphyllia shares the same husbandry family.

additional.name: WoRMS – Polyphyllia talpina (AphiaID 211418)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Fungiidae, Order Scleractinia, confirmed accepted name Polyphyllia talpina Lamarck 1801), synonyms (Fungia talpina, Herpetolitha talpina), native range (Indo-West Pacific: Australia, Indonesia, Philippines, India, Japan, Papua New Guinea, Maldives, Madagascar, Mozambique), habitat description ('reef slopes, especially where Fungia is common'), and maximum wild size (75 cm per FAO/Carpenter & Niem 1998). Not used for aquarium husbandry parameters.
